does: This paper introduces ReproRepo, a framework for evaluating how well LLM agents can identify and help resolve reproducibility issues in machine learning research. Rather than relying on manual curation, the framework leverages naturally occurring GitHub issues raised by users as supervision signals on real-world reproduction blockers. The authors apply ReproRepo to 1,149 recent ML papers from major conferences and benchmark four frontier model-agent configurations. Their key finding is that LLM agents without code execution capabilities can identify semantically related human-reported blockers for approximately 90% of papers when using the best configuration (Codex with GPT-4.5). The analysis reveals that agents excel at surfacing visible failures and pinpointing relevant semantic regions within codebases, though they show limitations in certain problem categories.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to benchmark LLM agents' capabilities in assisting with scientific reproducibility, extending prior benchmarks that required labor-intensive manual curation and evaluation. It connects to the broader literature on reproducibility crises in machine learning and computational science, where code availability and replicability remain persistent challenges. The approach leverages insights from automated software engineering and program synthesis, where LLMs have shown promise in understanding code semantics and identifying bugs. The use of GitHub issues as naturally occurring supervision draws from work on mining software repositories and learning from real-world user feedback, offering a scalable alternative to hand-crafted evaluation datasets.
enables: This framework opens pathways for scaling reproducibility assessment across the entire ML research landscape without proportional increases in manual effort. By demonstrating that agents can identify real blockers from paper-code pairs, the work suggests future systems could automatically triage and prioritize reproducibility issues for researchers. The findings point toward developing more sophisticated agent configurations that move beyond semantic identification toward actual execution and resolution of reproduction failures. ReproRepo could enable longitudinal studies tracking reproducibility trends across time and venues, or be extended to other scientific domains. The work also motivates research into improving agent capabilities specifically for reproducibility tasks, including better handling of complex failure modes and integration with actual code execution environments for end-to-end reproduction.
